[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so here we go with a new Magic Monday. The picture is of the inimitable Helena Petrovna Blavatsky, co-founder and moving spirit of the Theosophical Society, who played a larger role than any other individual in kickstarting the modern revival of occultism in the English-speaking world, and who was also about half the reason you've heard of a place called Atlantis (and very nearly the entire reason you've heard of a place called Lemuria). 

Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer, though it may be Tuesday sometime before I get to them all. If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 712,254th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button below to access my online tip jar.

With that said, have at it! 

***This Magic Monday is now closed to new questions. See you next week!***

comments.content:
1) I still keep getting a weird feeling whenever I do anything occult and it works. Am I correct to view this as a result of the environment I grew up in and needing to unlearn the material world I grew up in?

2) I have an astrology question: last week, someone asked about a stellium square another stellium, and you said squares can be an immense source of creative power if the sides can be balanced. Do you have advice on how to get that balance?

3) One pattern I'm noticing a lot is that people don't seem to be aware of how much time they waste online and watching TV. Is this a result of the glamour, or is it something else?

4) I've been slowly gaining past life memories for a while, and I now have a fairly good understanding of who I was two lives, three lives, and five lives back, although my life right before is blurry save for a handful of incidents, I only remember how four back died and nothing else about that life. I'm now getting glimpses of a life during Roman times.

I suspect that I wouldn't spend more than thousand years out of incarnation and then come back in the 1700s and have this many lives in quick succession, so this raises a few questions:

4a) What determines how easy it is to remember particular past lives?

4b) How common is it to have crystal clear memories of certain lives, but then hardly anything from others?

4c) Is there any way to accelerate the recovery of memories of a particular lifetime? I feel like four back is rather important, although since I know next to nothing of that life, it's hard to tell.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Yep. Your culture has bombarded you since you were born with propaganda insisting that this stuff doesn't exist and that you're wrong and bad if you experience it, and that takes time and experience to outgrow.

2) Depends on the stelliums, and also on the personal factor. The one piece of advice that seems generally useful is to recognize that both factors in the square deserve a place in your life.

3) Good question. I suspect it's mostly the glamour, but I don't know for sure.

4a) It depends on what you're ready to remember.

4b) Very common.

4c) Let the memories surface when they're ready to surface. It's hard but important to trust the process.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:

4a) Is it the higher self that makes these decisions? If so, I wonder why it/I'm making the decisions I am. They seem rather arbitrary/nonsensical to me. I suppose that may also have to do with me being tiny and not havign the capacity to understand it.

4b) This would seem to imply it's possible that even most people standing on the verge of Gwynvudd are unable to remember certain past lives. Not in the sense they are blurry, but in the sense that they can't remember them at all. Huh. I suspect I need to think this through a great deal more.

4c) There are quite a few cases where I feel like life would've been much easier had I known about details from my past lives earlier. I guess it's the learning experience that I need though, to the point where I don't need the past life memories to know certain things are worth doing and others worth avoiding.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
3) I won't argue!

4a) Yes, exactly. Just remember that your brain is only six inches long...

4b) Yes, that's what it does imply. It's one of the classic signs of actually breaking through to Gwynfydd that you remember them all -- when Taliesin in his great song of triumph said "I have been all things previously," that's what was involved.

4c) Exactly. The important thing is that the lessons have been internalized in your character, so you don't need to remind yourself.

replies.content:
Still another question about the complex subject of reincarnation: If a certain kind of people doesn't have other interests than eating, sleeping, having sex and watching TV, how can it than be that the souls of these people get more complex in a sequence of lifetimes? I don't see which forces could precipitate such an development if said people are content with the abovementioned activities.

Another question: It has occurred to me that the sequence of human lifetimes might be analogous to the way an individual human develops from baby via toddler, child and adolescent to an grown-up person and so on. Is that a good analogy?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Your interests in life are far from the only source of experiences you have. The work you have to do to make a living (or the intricacies of getting money from the welfare system if you happen to live in a place that has one), the interactions you have with other people, the various sources of pain and grief and suffering that affect you, and much more -- all these things have their impact. Remember also that someone who is content with just being alive may be in his or her first life as a human being, and thus simply trying to make sense of being human is a full-time job.

2) It's a workable analogy but a somewhat simplistic one, because most souls end up going through trajectories that are much less linear than that.

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)

replies.content:
Dear JMG,

I was speaking with my wife yesterday about some of the things in the Druid Magic Handbook, which I am very close to embarking upon, and I tried to introduce to her some of the ideas in it, because if I'm going to start down that path I want to be open with her about it.

I am greatly simplifying below her more subtle points, but she is usually skeptical about such systems for various reasons, not least because the inability of verifying the truth of them means that they are open to abuse by those who wish to control others and to keep power structures in place (about karma, she used the example of the caste system in India, which is justified by the idea of karma, that you are stuck in your place for karmic reasons). Given that it is impossible, in her view, to verify that reincarnation exists, that karma exists, that astrology works, etc, why bother blindly following a tradition that assumes these things? She is unfamiliar with your writings on these topics and how you're brought them into the open. She also has a problem with the idea of doing 'good' things for reasons of karma, in that ultimately one does these good things for selfish reasons rather than doing them because they are intrinsically good. She is bringing some of my own doubts back in foreground, including the seemingly arbitrary nature of certain spiritual traditions.

Rather coincidentally, later the same day I attended a music workshop led by a teacher who I believe comes from a Buddhist background, who tries to encourage in these workshops a style of improvisation which comes not from aiming to fit a specific musical style, but rather from close listening. In the workshops, one listens to what the other musicians are doing, and improvising something which the music itself needs, and not from your own preconceptions ideas about what 'should' happen. The idea behind it, it seems, is that this type of improvisation is based on dealing with the question of uncertainty: that one can never be totally certain of anything except for death, and if I understand him right, one way of dealing with that is by observing what is already present and responding to it ‘authentically’. The teacher is using the activity of music-making as a metaphor for the rest of life.

And then this morning I re-read a short parable from Alan Watts, which suddenly jumped out in my memory in response to all this, entitled The Second Immortal, in which a cakeseller who lives an ordinary life and who enjoys ordinary activities goes off, encouraged by a priest, to find an immortal who can tell him truths. He meets wise men who encourage him to read all of the classics, and to eat less and breath well, etc, neither of which brings him any closer to what he's after. He finally meets an immortal, who in turn shows the cakeseller that by doing the ordinary things he was originally doing, he was already an immortal himself. Very Taoist, I guess. Watts has written elsewhere about that first passage in the Tao Te Ching in which the Way as described is not the real Way, and that one can't help but live in the Way because it's impossible to be outside of it, etc etc.

Forgive me if all these thoughts seem muddled, but at heart they are different ways of looking at the same question: how to deal with uncertainty, and the self-paralysis that results in me from that. Following the system of magic as outlined in the DMH is something I am becoming increasingly drawn to, but I'm wondering whether I will be like the cakeseller, and end up spending time at something without getting any closer to 'truth'. I don't totally know yet exactly what I mean by truth here, I just know that I increasingly feel drawn to these sorts of questions, given how often I think about them, but at the same time I just end up in confusion and inaction.

I'm sure these are things that you have encountered before, and I'd appreciate any guidance you might offer.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
(It's fine to post it here.)

These are all things that you, personally, need to sort out. Not everyone is suited to magical training, and if you aren't sure it's for you, then it's perfectly appropriate to do something else with your life. My usual advice to people who are considering taking up magic, but aren't sure, is to suggest that they do something else with their lives if they can. It's those who can't walk away from it who have what it takes to tackle the immense challenges of the Path.

As for your wife's arguments, it's always possible to raise such objections to any branch of knowledge that depends on sources of information not immediately accessible to everyone. The prevalence of creationism is a good example of this -- you can't prove the reality of evolution to them, because it's necessary to learn how to read the record of fossil forms and the subtle patterns of biology, and those who don't learn this can always insist that those who do are just blindly following arbitrary assumptions. If you engage in occult practice, you'll find out in due time exactly why the teachings say what they do -- and if you don't, why, then you won't.

Two questions: the first is how do you go about communicating with the higher self? From what I understand it's me, but at the same time it's not quite me as I usually think of it. Would prayer to my higher self be advisable, or is there a different way to relate to it?

Second: you provided a working to call down all karma on oneself. I have a massive problem in I run away from things. This has been true of several lives now, and I want to fix this. Calling down all my karma seems like a drastic step, so is there a safer way to go about this? If it helps, I am working my way through the Dolmen Arches course (currently on Grade II)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Most of any serious system of magical spirituality is aimed at opening up the connection between your lower self -- the personality you think of as "yourself" -- and your higher self -- the enduring spiritual reality of which your lower self is a temporary projection. (In the cosmology of the Dolmen Arch, these are called the hunan and the elaeth respectively. Bringing the two into contact with one another is far from easy; prayer is one traditional way to go about it, and the regular practice of magic and meditation will also move in that direction, but don't expect instant results (and if you get what look like instant results, be very careful, because the hunan is very good at tricking itself.)

2) In case I didn't make it clear at the time, I do not recommend that anybody do that working! If you want to change the way you relate to the world, the first thing you need to do is clear your enaid (the body of life-energy or nwyfre) of the various kinds of crud that get stuck to it, and to strengthen your will. Daily practice of the Sphere of Protection will do both. As that works, you'll find opportunities to begin to sort through why you run away from things. Meditation is useful here, and so is journaling (as covered in the Magic Monday FAQ). You need to understand a pattern like that before you can outgrow it.

As Pluto finishes waning between now and 2036, I expect a lot of those factors to become much less important than they've been, and people in the future will be just as baffled by the popularity of William Burroughs, say, as most people are now by The Sorrows of Young Werther.

replies.title: Re: Pluto/Scorpio
replies.content: Heh, I wonder how Norman Mailer will fare. I was always fascinated by his dark occult-tinged themes, which I suspect, were intuitive on his part. Critics seemed to regard Mailer as a more legitimate literary figure than Burroughs, but they dismissed his attraction to macho-metaphysics and occultism as a bug - for me, it was a feature. And those were some dark themes indeed. He really let loose in his tremendously fun semi-pulpy mystery novel Tough Guys Don't Dance.

replies.title: Re: Pluto/Scorpio
replies.content: What caused Pluto and Ceres to briefly become planets? Is this a fairly odd thing, or is it just that we noticed these two, but historically we didn't see other "minor planets"? I ask since I can't find any records of extra planets during Roman times/Middle Ages.

replies.title: Re: Pluto/Scorpio
replies.content: It's not a common thing as far as we know. The last big rush of planetary discovery was about five thousand years ago, and coincided (so to speak) with the birth of urban society, literacy, and the rest of civilization; as far as I know, nobody knows whether any temporary planets were discovered in those days -- though for a good long time Venus was thought to be two planets, one as the morning star and one as the evening star. In modern times, two permanent planets (Uranus and Neptune) were discovered; several temporary planets have been discovered and then downgraded; and then there are the planets that were proposed but never quite discovered -- Vulcan and the "black moon" Lilith are the best known of these. All of them represent new forces, some of which established themselves permanently in our cosmos and our psyches, some of which were temporary presences, and some of which never got the foothold they were expected to have, and faded out without ever quite constellating into definite forces.

replies.title: Re: 'Celtic' Astrology

comments.title: SOP variations and their significance
comments.content:
John,

Thanks for offering this opportunity for questions. Since I've gone even a little bit beyond reading your books and blog posts, I've been swarmed with them. For now, just a few closely related ones:

1) I've seen several ways to do the Sphere of Protection by now, with two of them on the AODA website at different times. Are they all effective? AODA's old version from early 2018 says, "You're free to read these instructions and use your own symbols. You're free to alter these instructions to suit your own purposes. Ritual is individual because Magick is individual. Ritual is universal because a lot of people use the same symbols. You get to decide how you want to do your own rituals." This and other similar statements give me a sense of great flexibility, but I suspect that at least part of ritual has to be consistent across practitioners for it to be a distinct, working ritual (doesn't it?). So in a sense, I'm wondering, where are the limits, the parts of the ritual that can't be altered without diminished or ill effect? Does the DMH, which I intend to find, go into that degree of under-the-hood detail?

a) Is the sphere of light in the current AODA version functionally the same as circulating the light?

b) AODA's old explanation suggested that Earth was associated with physical circumstances like one's health or finances, while now its description doesn't mention these but talks about steadfastness and staying the course. Is one or both or neither of these accurate? How flexible are elemental associations here?

c) Unrelated to "how not to break the ritual," I've been looking into the Irish deities lately--with whom is air typically associated in that pantheon?

replies.title: Re: SOP variations and their significance
replies.content:
1) The Sphere of Protection is a very flexible ritual. The things to leave intact are the basic patterns -- invoking four powers in the Elemental Cross, tracing the distinctive emblems in the seven directions and invoking and banishing as instructed, and so on. The Druid Magic Handbook doesn't go into great detail about the underlying logic, but you can follow the instructions and figure out what can be varied and what can't.

a) I'm not a great fan of the "sphere of light;" it seems less effective to me. Still, your mileage may vary.

b) I'm also not a great fan of the purely psychological interpretation of the elements. They also have specific applications to your life, as health and finances.

c) William Butler Yeats, who was kind of an expert along these lines, assigned Brigid to air, Lugh to fire, Danu to water, and the Dagda to earth in his rituals for his Celtic magical order, The Castle of Heroes. If you can find suitable deities for Spirit Above and Spirit Below, that might be a good starting place.

replies.title: Re: SOP variations and their significance
replies.content: If I might jump in here, Aine or Grian would be suitable solar goddesses for the spirit above, and Nuada (the Irish cognate to the Brythonic Nodens) would make a fine telluric/spirit below deity.

replies.title: Re: SOP variations and their significance
replies.content: Thank you! The Irish traditions are not my strong suit, so I appreciate the suggestions.

replies.title: Re: SOP variations and their significance
replies.content:
Fascinating! So far (mostly) all I’ve been able to find concerning W.B.Y.’s Castle of Heroes is that it was centred around Loch Cé, and that it was ultimately somewhat abortive. Do you have recommendations for tracking down resources to learn more about what he did with it? The Irish gods being my primary focus of worship, I’d love to have a wider window into his occult insights as far as They are concerned.

-Scott H.

replies.title: Re: SOP variations and their significance
replies.content: All the Castle of Heroes material was published in a doctoral dissertation in 1977, "Yeats's Celtic Mysteries" by Lucy Shepard Kalogera. You can order a copy from ProQuest online here -- click through to the online store and run an author search for the full name, and it'll be right on top of the results.

replies.title: Re: SOP variations and their significance
replies.content:
Just to round out the three druids and five answers, I'll jump in here also. In working with the Irish deities in the SOP, I found the four treasures of the Tuatha de Danaan to be a helpful addition. Ross Nichols covers this a bit in his Book of Druidry.

It puts Lugh in Air with the Sword of Light, the Dagda in Water with his Cauldron. It doesn't specify which deities are assigned to fire and earth, but there are hints...

comments.title: Void of course
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Our family is having a debate. Is a prayer the same as a spell? Argument for says that you are using your will in order to manifest an action of some sort. Argument against says that you are asking for intercession by your favoured deity, not direct manifestation of your own will. We need a referee!

Leading on from this is the Moon's "void of course". Will prayers be answered if they are said during a void of course? Are we correct in assuming that void of courses affect workings?

Zed

replies.title: Re: Void of course
replies.content:
1) Prayers aren't spells. In a prayer, you say, "Thy will be done," and in a spell, you say, "My will be done." Not the same thing!

(Now it's only fair to say that people muck up the distinction. A lot of people who think they're praying spend their time telling God what to do, which is not what prayer is supposed to be about. Prayer is about communing with a deity, not handing over a list of goodies to Santa Claus.)

The Moon being void of course affects only magical workings that depend on astrological forces. You can do elemental magic just fine when the Moon is void, and you can also do spiritual magic under the same conditions -- that is to say, calling on deities, angels, etc. Prayer is unaffected by astrological conditions -- that's why so many traditions recommend it as a basic practice; you can do it at any time.)

replies.title: Re: Void of course
replies.content:
Many thanks for that.

Zed

comments.title: Casual Prenatal Yoga?
comments.content:
A variation on the children-and-magic theme from your faq.

Can yoga be done "casually"- ie, for physical exercise only, apart from its spiritual aspects?
If not, are children (born or unborn) put in any particular sort of risk if a mom attempts "casual" yoga with them?

I ask because of the growing presence of prenatal and "mommy and me" yoga (ie, classes where toddlers and moms are meant to both participate together). I notice it being offered in settings that are clearly meant for "casual" involvement: for instance, as part of a package of no-commitment, drop-in classes, or in online tutorials, or in free outdoor yoga-in-the-park group.

Thank you again for this platform and your insights!

replies.title: Re: Casual Prenatal Yoga?
replies.content:
Most yoga practiced in America today is nothing more than physical exercise, with at most a little light (as in featherweight) meditation or a bit of work on deep breathing. It's no more spiritual than jumping jacks and toe touches -- just more fashionable than these. That being the case, it should be fine for children, born or unborn.

Meditation is very good for pregnant women and mothers with young children, by the way, because it decreases the amount of fatigue toxins in the blood and breast milk, and also strengthens and cleanses the mother's energy body, which then has beneficial effects on the infant's energy body via resonance. Again, it's ***only*** ceremonial magic that's contraindicated.

replies.title: Re: Casual Prenatal Yoga?
replies.content: Thanks. I would imagine that yoga done properly is beneficial. I was more concerned about the superficial approach Americans have towards it. My father is from India, and my impression from this side of my family is that yoga ought not be approached as mere exercise. But I was unsure if that stems from a cultural/respectful position, or if there was another reason to frown on it (ie, unintended consequences from flippant use). My dad is quite secular and my grandmother, who was devout, is no longer around for me to ask such questions.

replies.title: Re: Casual Prenatal Yoga?
replies.content: The advantage of the casual, superficial approach to yoga is that it's just a form of exercise, and that's not going to harm anyone. It may not do much good, either, but that's what you get. ;-)

comments.content:
Hello JMG, I’m continuing with you book, The Art and Practice of Geomancy (APG). Before proceeding with my question, I want to relate how delighted I was with your response to my enquiry last week with respect to a geomantic take on the novel The last Unicorn. I have started taking your advice and am meditating upon geomantic interpretations of the characters. I have begun with The Unicorn herself, and appreciate a remarkable concordance with the figure Populus, so much so that I wonder if you were consciously thinking of The Unicorn when you were writing your section on Populus. I look forward to repeating this process with the other characters/figures, and wonder if the others will have such strong concordance ( I anticipate Schmendric and Puer will prove very strong).

I’m also delighted to hear that The Last Unicorn is worthy of deep reading. In my hopelessly clueless youth, I didn’t really know how to read novels. I basically approached the plot as if the author were analogous to a chess master setting up the board, then the end or climax of the novel was ‘checkmate’, and that was the nugget. It’s really only been in the last 10 years or so that I have realized that’s tantamount to not reading the novel at all, and it has only been in the last 2 years that I have finally begun to genuinely read a novel. I’ve been working mainly with Steppenwolf and The Last Unicorn (easy reads for you, as you've mentioned), and find it similar to diving into a tropical lagoon, going in for a bit to explore a small section, diving back in and exploring an adjacent section, starting to notice how one section connects to another, and also noticing little things on subsequent visits that escaped me on the first one or two visits. I’m reading the Last Unicorn for the fourth time now per your geomantic mediation suggestion, and am noticing and perceiving more than I ever have. I have just been trying to decide whether the Butterfly at the beginning could even be regarded even as a character, it seemed so elusive, remote and insubstantial, yet it reminded me of something that I couldn’t quite place. comments.title: polytheist middle pillar ritual
comments.content: I'm certainly willing to try-it-and-see, but what would be your opinion of using Arthurian names rather than god names? Thanks!

replies.title: Re: polytheist middle pillar ritual
replies.content:
I don't recommend it. Partly, one of the reasons you're using divine names is that you're calling on actual, living spiritual powers, not archetypes or people who lived a long time ago and are dimly remembered in legend -- but there's another factor...

The Arthurian legends are a story of failure. What gives them their pathos is the vision of something truly great that nearly succeeded in coming into manifestation, and then unraveled catastrophically. Embody those images and you embody that failure. That's why every attempt to use the core Arthurian myth as the basis for a system of initiation has ended up going to bits after a certain period -- it's hardwired in the imagery and legendry.

replies.title: Re: polytheist middle pillar ritual
replies.content: This seems like a potential issue for Druids, as Arthurian symbolism plays a prominent role in some branches...

replies.title: Re: polytheist middle pillar ritual
replies.content: Yep. You can use the Arthurian imagery in certain ways, and some of the legends that have become tacked onto the Arthurian legendarium don't have the same issue -- for example, the legends of Merlin, Taliesin, and the Grail don't inevitably drag you down. Still it's important to be careful.

replies.title: Re: polytheist middle pillar ritual
replies.content:
A while back, I read The Secret Tradition in Arthurian Legend, as it's listed in the Druidry Handbook bibliography.

Would someone using the images and practices discussed in Knight's book run into the wall you're talking about? Or was Knight able to figure out a way around it?

Also, the DMH uses the images of Excalibur and the Grail, but I'm guessing these are symbols of eternal spiritual realities, and so lack the frailty of the human characters of the Grail Cycle.
-Cliff

replies.title: Re: polytheist middle pillar ritual
replies.content:
Knight's book is based on material that was used for a while by Dion Fortune's Society of the Inner Light, and it was fortunately never central to the SIL's work. I know of several attempts to use Knight's book as the basis for a system of initiation, and they all crashed and burned.

As for the DMH, the Grail legend in its oldest and purest forms is a legend of triumph rather than failure -- the Grail is recovered, the Waste Land is healed --

"I have made my ceremonies; the crisis comes.
None may reproduce, no waters flow,
Until the sword is from its sheath pulled.
Only I, the medicine-man Gawain, instructing
At midnight Galahad, the pure one of spring --
I alone can save you, faithless with the fallen leaf.
I prove to you once again, O my dead people,
That I alone am your strength."


-- and Excalibur is also part of that pattern, as the verses by Ross Nichols I've quoted here demonstrate. It's not the Grail cycle but the core Arthurian legend of the rise and fall of Arthur and Camelot that has failure hardwired into it.

replies.title: Re: polytheist middle pillar ritual
replies.content:
Fascinating! The same failure underlies everything in the musical "Camelot" (1960), which opened in 1960, took the country by storm, was JFK's favorite bedtime listening, and was literally omnipresent throughout American popular culture through the rest of the 1960s. It almost felt like an initiation, a rebirth that the country was experiencing in those years.

And here you write "That's why every attempt to use the core Arthurian myth as the basis for a system of initiation has ended up going to bits after a certain period -- it's hardwired in the imagery and legendry." From many points of view the USA certainly did "go to bits" as the national consensus of the '40s and '50s broke down spectacularly during the '60s and '70s.

replies.title: Re: polytheist middle pillar ritual
replies.content: Yes, exactly -- and that's also, I think, an important part of why the Sixties counterculture crashed and burned the way it did.

replies.title: Re: polytheist middle pillar ritual
replies.content:
As someone who was an adult at the time, it always seemed to me that the cultural crash-and-burn of the later '60s and the '70s wasn't limited to the counter-culture. The mainstream culture (as I knew it in the '40s and '50s) also crashed and burned at the same time, though not so spectacularly.

The culture that emerged from the wreckage at the end of the '70s, and took hold of the whole nation in the '80s, seemed to me at the time to be a new and radical break with the past. And it also seemed to me to be a highly toxic, vicious, cruel thing--repellent on the visceral level. (That's a large part of why I swore off TV around 1985.)

To be sure, that emergent culture did take on some things from the older mainstram culture, and a few other things from the dying counter-culture, but these seemed like mere trappings, mere theatrical props and costumes as a new cast of actors took the stage to present a new play to the populace.

comments.title: embodiment
comments.content:
Hi JMG

I have observed a certain part of the Neopagan scene where a priestess ‘embodies’ the Goddess. I went to 2 of them and I’m not sure that the Goddess is really able to ‘take over’ during an embodiment. I think it might be the charisma (or lack of it) of the individual that is being expressed.

Was this done in ancient times and is it safe? I think for immature priestesses in particular, there could be a real risk of inflation, ie, thinking that they are goddesses outside the embodiment.
I know you think that the current fad for Wicca will die down. How long has it got? I presume old fashioned Druidry and occultism will continue in their own fringe way…

Thanks.

replies.title: Re: embodiment--Wiccan weighing in
replies.content:
Generally speaking, the ideal for the 'embodiment' aka "Drawing Down the Moon" portion of the Wiccan ritual is a light mediumship in which the Goddess may 'glamorize' the priestess to some degree and may speak through her with some general or specific message about the meaning of the season, problems ahead, the magical working being done, etc. In some covens the priestess is prepared to recite a general goddess speech, such as Doreen Valiente's "Charge of the Goddess" if no inspiration is forthcoming. Or she may speak some general words of welcome, celebration, uplift. Some covens, particularly in initiatory traditions such as Gardnarian teach specific techniques to bring on the trance. Much depends on the ability of the priestess to open herself to the goddess. Of course it is possible for coveners to respond to the personal charisma of the priestess, but recall that a special gift from God was the original meaning of charisma and its current use to mean merely a strong personality is a downgrade. In any case, the embodiment ends when the Circle is over. Some rituals contain specific wording asking the Goddess to step back out so that her priestess can eat and drink with the coven as one of them. No one is supposed to go through their normal day thinking that they are manifesting the goddess. If this happens, we all start edging away.

In some traditions the God is drawn down at particular Sabbats--this is related to the cycle of solar holidays. The same conditions apply.

I got involved in Wicca over 40 years ago. I am an elder in a Central Valley (of California) British tradition and also in a Gardnarian lineage. Also have experience in an eclectic California group. I have heard of priestesses who were manipulative, using 'messages from the goddess' to get their way, but it doesn't usually last long and the increased communication between groups has made it more difficult for abusive leaders of any group to hang onto students. It is hard to pretend to be the only Witch in town when a Google search will prove you wrong.

Hope this illuminates the question.

Rita

replies.title: Re: embodiment
replies.content:
It's basically a form of mediumship, and suffers from all the usual problems and uncertainties of mediumship -- on the one hand, self-deception, and on the other hand deliberate dishonesty. Those aren't inevitable by any means, and I'm told there are Wiccan priestesses who do "drawing down" very well and get good results for themselves and their coveners, but it's not something I would want to have anything to do with. (Of course I'm not a Wiccan.)

Pop eclectic Wicca is already losing ground and I expect it to shed most of its current followers by 2025 or so. Old-fashioned British Traditional Wicca is another matter and is probably around for the fairly long term. Druidry, ditto -- that's three hundred years old at this point and very resilient. As for occultism, that's as old as our species and shows no sign of flagging yet!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Since it's a yes/no question, it's best to interpret a chart like this using the modes of perfection, which give you a crisp and definite answer in such cases. Omens belong to the ninth house of divination, so your figure is Carcer; your parents are the fourth house, so Fortuna Minor; and the owl is Rubeus. No mode of perfection connects Rubeus and Carcer, or Rubeus and Fortuna Minor, so the answer is "Sometimes an owl is just an owl."

Owls, by the way, have a positive side as well. They're a traditional emblem of wisdom, and of course they also catch rats!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
John--

Three questions, if I may:

1) Re Madame Blavatsky, what order would you recommend for tackling her works and/or those related to her teachings?

2) How would you distinguish between Wisdom, Understanding, and Knowledge? (Chokmah, Binah, Daath?)

3) If Daath is the shadow cast upon the Great Abyss by the sephiroth of the supernal triad (Kether-Consciousness, Chokmah-Force, Binah-Form), which I picture as an inverted tetrahedron, what is at the apex of the mirror (upright) tetrahedron providing the light by which that shadow is generated? (This would seem to me to be the location of the Demiurge/First Principle which was generated by the Unmanifest, before it broke into the three elements?)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Pick up Geoffrey Barborka's The Divine Plan or some other basic intro to Theosophical ideas, and start with that so you have some idea of what you're getting into when you tackle The Secret Doctrine!

2) Knowledge is separate from the knower and the known; you have four things in the act of knowledge: the knower, the known, the act of knowing, and the knowledge gained. (Relate this to the Tetragrammaton.) Understanding has only three things: the person who understands, the thing that is understood, and the act of understanding -- there's no separate "understandage" gained outside of the act of understanding. Finally, wisdom has only two elements; the wise person and the wisdom. You don't "wise" something; it's something you are, not something you do. So 4, then 3, then 2 -- I'll let you relate those to the Tree of Life all by yourself. ;-)

3) Are you sure the geometrical model you're using is relevant? The First Principle is Kether, not some uber-Kether above Kether. Above Kether you find only the three veils of the Unmanifest, Ain (Nothing), Ain Soph (Limitless), and Ain Soph Aur (Limitless Light), which are not any kind of apex but the vague mumblings we use to try to talk about the inexpressible.

replies.title: Re: House cleansing

Get a brand new mop and a brand new bucket. Put water into the bucket, and add a cup of vinegar, a quarter cup of kosher salt, and a quarter teaspoon of cayenne pepper. comments.title: (no subject)

JMG-

I'm trying to figure out what magical traditions might have been a part of my family before my grandfather's parents came to America. My family is from the Budapest area and fled to America at the start of WWI.

Do you have any idea what sort of ritual magical practices might have been common in Budapest region circa 1900? Or ideas on how I might go about figuring that out? (I do not speak or read Hungarian, but several relatives do, so I could potentially use non-English sources)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Central Europe has an extraordinarily rich tradition of magic, very little of which is known on this side of the Atlantic. Around 1900, you'd have had dozens of different traditions ranging from Magyar folk magic and Renaissance occultism through Rosicrucian and Behmenist traditions to early Theosophy and Martinism. It's not something I've looked into to any great degree, as I don't have any personal connection there, but Budapest and the old Austro-Hungarian Empire generally was a seething cauldron of different magical traditions. You might see if you can find a good biography of Rudolf Steiner -- he grew up in late 19th century central Europe and made connections with some of the various traditions that were active then.

Anyway, what I have struggled with is the word "consciousness" in that context, because at first sight it seems very personal, very inward-looking, as if the changes are only in oneself (not that that is a bad thing.) So that we see and experience something we would not have necessarily seen and experienced otherwise?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Yes, that's one way to approach it. Keep in mind that the universe at every moment is offering you many possibilities, positive and negative, and which of them you perceive depends entirely on your state of consciousness. One very effective way to get rich, for example, is to focus your consciousness on seeing opportunities to make money. If you see every dollar that comes your way as a tool to make more money, and every chance at earning money that you encounter catches your eye and makes you think, "Is that the best way for me to earn more, or can I apply the same idea somewhere else?" -- well, you can fill in the blanks yourself. Equally, if you want to be miserable, it's just as easy to focus your consciousness on seeing reasons to be miserable -- and in fact many people do this quite systematically.

Your state of consciousness orients you toward some things and away from others. Choose what you want to focus on, and the results follow...

replies.title: Re: Geomancy ans Health
replies.content: Oh, I'd do geomancy. I'd start by casting a general house chart for my health, note the figure in the sixth house as an overview, see if it passes to any other house of the chart, and use that as a guide to the nature of the illness -- Rubeus in the sixth and the second, for example, suggests an inflammatory condition in the throat (the second house is the natural house of Taurus, which rules the throat).  Then I'd cast another chart about whatever treatment I was considering, remembering that in a medical chart, the tenth house is the medicine or treatment, and the figures in the first and sixth would tell me how the treatment would affect me generally and the illness. I'd follow with more readings as needed. Yes, you can ask "is this a good idea" questions, or anything else that can be answered with a yes or a no.

Basically, for those on a path of spiritual growth, personal growth, etc, magic per Dion Fortune's definition will occur, although often by the use of differing boxes of tools. For some, via study and trying to gain wisdom, for others, via ritual magic, and for others via methods of the mystic. Also, the sage, the mage and the mystic will all use meditation of various types, including but not limited to discursive. My meditations on runes, for example, often start more as chanting and envisioning the stave before any discursive elements occur, which tool I believe you've mentioned regarding symbolism in general and the Ogham in particular. A lot of observation and studying and thinking occur by all three "types" also. The scholarship and nature observations you wrote about in "The Druidry Handbook" really get at these ideas.


The conclusion at which I'm arriving is that the sage, the mage and the mystic all use similar tools and sometimes overlap into what on the surface is the occupation of one of the other "types". In other words, there is a LOT of overlap, very fuzzy rather than defined limits and activities of each "type", and that the healthy practitioner will pursue elements of all three so as to keep the practitioner balanced. Overemphasizing one aspect over the others will lead to problems. I've known people who were so into their mystic experiences that they had limited ability to turn on a light switch. It might also be argued that Crowley so overemphasized his ritual magic, that his life became a dumpster fire as a result.

In other words, on these paths, then, most of us will need to have some abilities as a sage, some as a mage and some as a mystic. The balance will differ for each person. The label that one then refers to oneself may be inconsequential to some.

Or am I totally barking up the wrong tree?

DJSpo

comments.title: DMH Cranebag

So my question is in three parts:

2. Given what I currently use to hold my Ogham and the shoulder bag that I use, should I enchant one of those two tools? Or should I obtain yet another bag that I do the enchantment on that meets the criteria described in the book?

When doing the ritual for the wand and for the cauldron, each of these tools are used in the ritual in certain ways to cement the enchantment. What function is the Cranebag serving in this phase that is helping to cement its enchantment, since it is not actively used?

replies.title: Re: DMH Cranebag
replies.content:
1) No, everything in the system is there in the book. The crane bag is there primarily as a lamen.

2) You should get a separate bag that meets the criteria in the book. Those aren't there for show.

3) It's not an active tool. The wand is the active tool, the crane bag the passive tool, the cauldron the meeting ground between active and passive, thus giving you the typical Druid threefold balance. The crane bag is being present and receptive in the ritual; that's its function.

comments.title: Dolmen Arch, re: Planets, and Ceremonies of Transformation

2) There has been discussion here a few times before regarding potentials for further development of the Dolmen Arch tradition, specifically regarding invisibility and animal transformation:

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/24201.html?thread=1169289#cmt1169289
https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/27474.html?thread=1487954#cmt1487954
https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/40156.html?thread=2583004#cmt2583004

I've never performed Golden Dawn magic, but looking at the Z-2 rituals I don't see what differences in them matter. It seems a straightforward thing to extract the ritual formulae from the Grove Ceremony and the Sphere of Protection, and adapt them to the purposes just as the formula of the Ritual of the Enterer was adapted in the Golden Dawn. Both versions would still define a sacred space, prime the magician for the energies to be invoked, and then invoke the energies and aid of the (four elements/seven cantrefs). replies.title: Re: Dolmen Arch, re: Planets, and Ceremonies of Transformation
replies.content:
1) Not very close. It's simply a bit of additional symbolism; I wouldn't rely on it too much.

2) What you're missing is that the Z-2 rituals are based on ceremonies of initiation, and that's what gives them their special power. You would have to base the ritual on an initiation ritual -- for example, the First Degree ritual of AODA -- in the same way that the Z-2 rituals are based on the GD Neophyte grade, so that you are literally initiating a magical force, in the full ritual sense of that word.

3) I don't have a specific time frame for The Dolmen Arch but I've been told it's in the final stages of layout. I'd expect it in print sometime this spring.

comments.title: Astology

Your recent ingress chart interpretations over the past year have inspired me to start looking back into the astrology I started to learn may moons ago and then put aside "to get back to someday".
One of the questions that I found puzzling at the time, and still do, runs like this: NASA and ESA and the entire astronomical science department of every university can pinpoint the location of any given star by azimuth and declination on the celestial sphere, and we have superbly accurate longitude and latitude co-ordinates for every place on Earth, even accounting for the slight deviation from a perfect sphere, and the astronomy sciences have long been able to co-ordinate observations between these two spheres to hundredths of a degree of accuracy for times of fractions of a second. When I check the azimuth and declination of any given planet, as viewed from the location of my birthplace at the time of my birth, every single scientific database gives me exactly the same answer. But, why does every astrological ephemeris that I can find online or in the library give me a different value for planetary positions and signs for my birthchart? Same data, different results.

Bruce Hearns (AKA Renaissance Man)

replies.title: Re: Astology
replies.content: Are you just looking things up in an ephemeris, or are you actually calculating the birth chart? If the former, then no wonder, because you can't get your correct birth data just by looking things up in an ephemeris -- you have to do the math.  If the latter, you're doing something wrong, because the same birth data should produce the same birth chart down to as many decimal points as your ephemeris goes.

replies.title: Re: Astology
replies.content:
I calculated my birth chart. I've also had birth charts done by three professional astrologers.
They don't match up. The aspects are mostly the same, but the location of planets in houses and signs -- and therefore the interpretations -- are quite different in each chart.
One chart and interpretation is a very close description of me, the others are somewhat less so.
For example:
I've moved about, so I've lost most of the calculations I did for my chart, along with my chart, but the scraps I've got left are two pages of calculations that conclude that the standard ascendant on my chart is at 0 degrees 56 minutes of Capricorn.
The professional chart that comes closest to describing me has the ascendant at 0 degrees 39 minutes of Capricorn.
Another professional chart puts the ascendant at 17 degrees, 41 minutes of Sagittarius.
I'm confused.
Shouldn't everyone, working from the same invariant data, be producing the same results?
The reason I'm bugged about this is because I don't know whether I can trust my calculations, and the lack of consistency between different people who supposedly know what they are doing is doing nothing to instill confidence in my ability to learn this.
If astronomers and engineers in different countries at different times manage to put satellites into precise orbits, or land craft on the moon with great accuracy. Why can't two astrologers, given the exact same data, produce the same birth chart, and which one is more accurate?

replies.title: Re: Astology
replies.content:
At least two of the astrologers you consulted were incompetent. A competent astrologer should get the same ascendant as any other competent astrologer. In any worthwhile school of astrology, in fact, it's standard practice to give birth data to the students and have them erect the chart from scratch, and if they don't get it right, they do it over again until they figure out where they've made the mistake. For any birth time and place there is only one ascendant -- the degree, minute, and second of the ecliptic that is exactly on the plane of the horizon as seen from that time and place.

One of the downsides of the current status of astrology is that anybody can hang up their shingle and claim to be a professional, whether or not they have any training or experience. There are professional organizations that require proof of competence before they will certify you, but they don't have legal force, and not many people know about them. (Here in the US, the American Federation of Astrologers is the main certifying body.)

replies.title: Re: Astology
replies.content:
Anyway... Since I now see how I can get an online copy of the book on astrology you recommend (even if it *is* just a blurry .pdf of Llewellyn's The A to Z of Horoscopes) I am going to see if I can learn to do astrology from his book and keep the other charts as curiosities.

Bruce Hearns (AKA Renaissance Man)

replies.title: Re: Astology
replies.content: Delighted to hear it. You'll also need a good ephemeris and a book of house tables; I recommend The American Ephemeris for the 20th Century by Neil F. Michelsen -- there's also a volume for the 21th century -- and The American Book of Tables, both published by ACS Publications. You might also find James A. Eshelman's Horoscope Calculation useful -- it goes into the astronomical and mathematical basis of astrology in quite some detail.

replies.title: Re: Astology
replies.content: Excellent, thank you! Time to track down these books!
